trust.encryptionDesc: Industry-leading encryption for data in transit and at rest
trust.complianceDesc: Tamper prevention, timestamps, and audit logs built in
trust.legalDesc: Designed to meet the requirements of Japan's Electronic Signature Act
trust.auditDesc: Automatically generates certificates recording signer, time, and IP
trust.title: Security and legal validity
trust.subtitle: Hundredth Sign adopts industry-standard security technologies at an affordable price.

features.sectionSubtitle: Hundredth Sign includes the document workflows needed to send, sign, approve, and audit contracts.
features.highlightDesc: Upload a PDF, drag & drop signature fields, enter recipient emails, and send. No complex setup required.
features.createDesc: Upload PDFs and freely place signature and text fields. Save as templates for repeated use.
features.sendDesc: Just enter an email to send a signing request. Recipients don't need an account.
features.signDesc: Sign via typing, drawing, or image upload. Auto-generate Japanese-style round and square stamps.
features.stampDesc: Enter a name to auto-generate round and square stamps for Japanese business customs.
features.contactsDesc: Manage contacts centrally and select recipients with one click.
features.complianceDesc: PDF tamper prevention (flattening), timestamps, and audit logs for compliance. Auto-generated certificates.
features.i18nDesc: UI and email notifications in 4 languages. Send signing requests to overseas partners in their native language.
features.auditDesc: Record who signed, when, and from where. Auto-generate legally valid certificates of completion.

security.title: Self-hosted does not mean compromising security
security.subtitle: Hundredth Sign keeps security, auditability, and legal evidence in the core application.
security.tlsDesc: Highest-grade encryption for both data in transit and at rest. Prevents interception and tampering.
security.tamperDesc: Signed PDFs are flattened to prevent tampering. Technical guarantee of document authenticity.
security.lawDesc: Compliant with Japan's Electronic Signature Act and Electronic Bookkeeping Act for legally valid signatures.
security.globalDesc: With email notifications and signing screens in 4 languages, cross-border contracts are smooth and easy.
security.auditDesc: Records timestamps and IP addresses for all operations. Auto-generates completion certificates.
security.accessDesc: Set access codes for recipients to strengthen identity verification for sensitive contracts.
security.zeroAIDesc: Your contract data is never used for AI training. Data ownership always belongs to you.
security.bottomNote: Security, audit logs, and signed PDF evidence remain core parts of the OSS build.

faq.title: Frequently Asked Questions
faq.q1: Are Hundredth Sign's electronic signatures legally valid?
faq.a1: Hundredth Sign is designed to meet the requirements of Japan's Electronic Signature Act (witness-type) and Electronic Bookkeeping Act. All signatures include auto-generated certificates recording signer, time, and IP address. Please note that the final determination of legal validity depends on individual contract details and circumstances, so consult a legal professional if needed.
faq.q2: What changed in the OSS version?
faq.a2: The OSS version is a single-workspace, self-hosted application. Commercial hosted-service features, plans, quotas, and external OAuth login have been removed while core PDF signing, templates, staff accounts, and audit logs remain.
faq.a3: No. Signers don't need to register. They simply click the link in their email to sign. Works on PC or smartphone with just a browser.
faq.a4: No. Staff accounts are managed by the administrator. External recipients do not need accounts.
faq.q5: Can I use it with non-Japanese partners?
faq.a5: Yes. The UI supports multiple languages including Japanese and English, and email notifications support 4 languages. You can set notification language per recipient, sending signing requests in their native language.
faq.q6: What security measures are in place?
faq.a6: Communications are encrypted with TLS 1.3, stored data with AES-256. Signed PDFs are tamper-proofed (flattened), and all operations are recorded in audit logs. Access code authentication for signer verification is included as standard.

why.title: Control the contract workflow in your own environment.
why.titleSub: Self-hosted electronic contracts without vendor lock-in.
why.description: The OSS edition is designed for teams that want electronic signatures without vendor lock-in or hosted commercial features.
why.aiDesc: Run the application with your own database, storage, mail settings, and administrator accounts.
why.noSalesDesc: Plans, quotas, payment-provider logic, and currency logic are removed from the OSS build.
why.noPerSeatDesc: Customers receive email links and can view, sign, decline, and download documents without creating an account.
why.passOnDesc: PDF signing, templates, contacts, internal approvals, and WORM audit logs remain available.

comparison.title: Hosted service or self-hosted control.

cookieConsent.message: We use cookies to improve your experience. Essential cookies are required for the site to function.
cookieConsent.learnMore: Learn more in our Privacy Policy

acceptance.title: 1. Acceptance of Terms
acceptance.body: By accessing or using Hundredth Sign, you agree to be bound by these Terms of Service. If you do not agree to these terms, please do not use our service.

serviceDescription.title: 2. Service Description
serviceDescription.body: Hundredth Sign provides cloud-based electronic signature services for businesses. Our service enables users to send, sign, and manage legally binding electronic documents in compliance with applicable laws including the Act on Electronic Signatures and Certified Authentication Business (Japan).

electronicSignatures.title: 3. Legal Validity of Electronic Signatures
electronicSignatures.body: Electronic signatures created through Hundredth Sign are legally valid under the Act on Electronic Signatures and Certified Authentication Business (電子署名法) and the Act on Electronic Books (電帳法). Each signed document includes a tamper-evident audit trail with timestamps and cryptographic hashes.

userObligations.title: 4. User Obligations
userObligations.body: You are responsible for maintaining the confidentiality of your account credentials, ensuring the accuracy of information provided in documents, obtaining proper consent from all signing parties, and complying with all applicable laws and regulations.

prohibited.title: 5. Prohibited Activities
prohibited.body: You may not use Hundredth Sign for fraudulent purposes, to forge signatures or documents, to harass or harm others, or in any way that violates applicable laws.

intellectualProperty.title: 6. Intellectual Property
intellectualProperty.body: All intellectual property rights in Hundredth Sign, including software, design, and content, remain the property of the service provider. You retain all rights to your own documents and data.

privacy.title: 7. Privacy
privacy.body: Your use of Hundredth Sign is also governed by our Privacy Policy, which is incorporated into these Terms by reference.

limitation.title: 8. Limitation of Liability
limitation.body: To the maximum extent permitted by law, Hundredth Sign shall not be liable for any indirect, incidental, or consequential damages arising from your use of the service.

termination.title: 9. Termination
termination.body: We reserve the right to suspend or terminate your account if you violate these Terms. You may cancel your account at any time through the organization settings.

governingLaw.title: 10. Governing Law
governingLaw.body: These Terms shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of Japan. Any disputes shall be subject to the exclusive jurisdiction of the Tokyo District Court.

changes.title: 11. Changes to Terms
changes.body: We may update these Terms from time to time. We will notify you of significant changes via email or in-app notification. Continued use of the service after changes constitutes acceptance.

overview.title: 1. Overview
overview.body: Hundredth Sign is committed to protecting your privacy. This Privacy Policy explains how we collect, use, and safeguard your personal information in compliance with the Act on the Protection of Personal Information (個人情報保護法) of Japan and GDPR where applicable.

dataCollected.title: 2. Information We Collect
dataCollected.body: We collect information you provide directly (such as name, email address, and organization details), information generated through your use of the service (such as document metadata and audit logs), and technical information (such as IP addresses and browser type).

purpose.title: 3. How We Use Your Information
purpose.body: We use your information to provide and improve our services, send transactional emails (such as signature requests and notifications), comply with legal obligations, and prevent fraud and abuse.

thirdParty.title: 4. Third-Party Services
thirdParty.body: Operators may configure trusted third-party services for document storage, email delivery, and infrastructure. The OSS distribution does not require an external identity provider by default.

cookies.title: 5. Cookies
cookies.body: We use essential cookies for authentication and session management. We may also use analytics cookies to improve our service. You can manage your cookie preferences using the consent banner.

retention.title: 6. Data Retention
retention.body: We retain your account data for as long as your account is active. Signed documents and audit logs are retained for a minimum of 10 years to comply with the Electronic Books Preservation Act (電帳法). You may request deletion of your account data subject to legal obligations.

rights.title: 7. Your Rights
rights.body: You have the right to access, correct, or delete your personal data. EU residents have additional rights under GDPR including data portability and the right to object to processing. To exercise these rights, contact us at the address below.

security.title: 8. Security
security.body: We implement industry-standard security measures including AES-256 encryption at rest, TLS in transit, and WORM (Write Once Read Many) immutable audit logs.

contact.title: 9. Contact Us
contact.body: For privacy-related inquiries, please contact us at support@hundredthsign.com.

usage.title: Hundredth Sign Complete Manual
usage.description: A public manual for administrators, staff, external signers, internal approvers, operators, developers, integration owners, and AI/CI operators. It covers product boundaries, daily workflows, audit, API, CLI, DB, and FAQ.

ossNotice.title: OSS edition assumptions
ossNotice.body: This manual covers the OSS single-workspace edition. Commercial SaaS billing, tenant switching, and external customer account management are outside its scope.

search.label: Search this manual
search.placeholder: Examples: PDF, API key, migration, language
search.empty: No matching chapter. Try another word.

contactCta.title: When the manual does not resolve it
contactCta.body: Include the screen name, steps, time, document ID, and visible error. Do not send secrets.

notFound.title: Chapter not found
notFound.description: The requested manual chapter does not exist.
notFound.body: Choose another item from the chapter list or search.

intro.title: Introduction and OSS Product Boundary
intro.summary: Clarifies what the OSS edition includes, what commercial SaaS functions are excluded, and where public and authenticated pages differ.

boundary.title: Product scope
body: Hundredth Sign is an OSS electronic signature system for the Japanese market. It implements document upload, signature request workflows, internal approvals, audit logs, WORM-style evidence, locale-aware emails, REST API, and a CLI-friendly integration surface.
The OSS edition is a single-workspace product. A single operator organization owns staff, documents, contacts, templates, settings, API keys, and audit records.
Commercial SaaS features such as billing, plan management, tenant switching, multi-customer administration, and external customer accounts are intentionally outside this manual.
bullets: Use the public routes for signing, document viewing, approvals, legal pages, and this manual.
Use authenticated dashboard routes for daily staff operations.
Use API keys and signctl only for server-side or trusted automation contexts.
Never publish database URLs, API keys, session secrets, SMTP passwords, or storage credentials in tickets or screenshots.
Audit and system audit records are append-only operational evidence.
When behavior and documentation differ, treat the source code and product specification as the correction source and update this manual.
callouts: This page is public. It lists variable names and procedures, but it must never include real keys, connection strings, or customer data.

publicPrivate.title: Route groups
body: Routes are split by who can access them and what security boundary they represent.

authenticated.label: Authenticated routes
authenticated.detail: /dashboard and its child routes require staff authentication and are the normal working area for documents, inbox, contacts, templates, and settings.

operator.label: Operator setup
operator.detail: /setup is used only when the instance still needs bootstrap or operational setup.

integration.label: Integration routes
integration.detail: REST API endpoints and webhooks are not human UI pages; they are authenticated automation surfaces.

usersRoutes.title: Users, Roles, and Screens
usersRoutes.summary: Explains the differences between administrators, members, signers, CC recipients, internal approvers, and integration clients.

roles.title: Role definitions
body: Each actor has a different security boundary. Do not reuse a staff account or API key for a person or system that should have narrower access.

admin.label: Administrator
admin.detail: Manages staff, instance settings, templates, contacts, API keys, and audit review. Admins can perform high-impact configuration changes.

member.label: Member
member.detail: Creates documents, sends requests, checks dashboard tasks, manages assigned workflows, and uses contacts/templates according to permissions.

signer.label: External signer
signer.detail: Uses a tokenized signing link. Does not need a staff account. May sign, decline, or delegate if the request permits it.

cc.label: CC recipient
cc.detail: Receives visibility or completion notifications but is not a required signer unless also configured as a signer.

approver.label: Internal approver
approver.detail: Reviews internal approval requests and approves or rejects before the downstream workflow continues.

apiClient.label: External integration client
apiClient.detail: Uses API keys with explicit scopes. It is not a human user and all calls must be auditable.

routes.title: Screen map
body: Use this map to answer where an operation belongs before changing code or escalating a support request.

public.label: Public manual/legal/signing
public.detail: Manual, terms, privacy, contact, signing, document view, and approval token routes are public surfaces.

app.label: Dashboard
app.detail: Dashboard, documents, document detail, inbox, contacts, and templates are authenticated staff surfaces.

settings.label: Settings
settings.detail: Organization settings, staff, mail, language, API key, and audit-related settings belong to authenticated administrator surfaces.

api.label: API
api.detail: REST endpoints are for integrations and CI/AI operators. They require API key authentication and scope checks.

sign.label: Token pages
sign.detail: Signing and approval URLs identify the request by token, not by staff session.

setup.title: Initial Setup, Environment Variables, and Startup Conditions
setup.summary: Covers required and optional environment variables, startup migration checks, standard commands, and secret handling.

environment.title: Environment variables
body: Keep required values in .env or the hosting secret store. Commit only example names and safe defaults.
A production-like instance must have a database URL, session secret, application URL, mail delivery configuration, and storage/proxy settings appropriate to the deployment.

databaseUrl.label: DATABASE_URL
databaseUrl.detail: Required. MySQL connection string used by Drizzle migrations and the application server.

sessionSecret.label: SESSION_SECRET
sessionSecret.detail: Required in real deployments. Used to protect staff authentication sessions.

appUrl.label: APP_URL / PUBLIC_APP_URL
appUrl.detail: Required for links in email, signing URLs, approval URLs, and API/CLI output.

smtp.label: SMTP_*
smtp.detail: Required for real email delivery. Local development may use a mail catcher or disabled delivery.

storage.label: Storage and PDF proxy variables
storage.detail: Required when uploaded PDFs or signed PDFs are stored outside the local filesystem.

openai.label: OPENAI_API_KEY
openai.detail: Optional. Only required for features that call OpenAI APIs. Do not reuse keys from unrelated projects.

env.title: Example .env names
env.code:
DATABASE_URL=mysql://user:password@host:3306/sign
SESSION_SECRET=replace-with-random-secret
APP_URL=https://example.invalid
SMTP_HOST=smtp.example.invalid
SMTP_PORT=587
SMTP_USER=replace-with-user
SMTP_PASS=replace-with-password
OPENAI_API_KEY=optional-only-when-ai-features-are-enabled

callouts: Never paste real DATABASE_URL, SMTP_PASS, SESSION_SECRET, or API keys into the manual, screenshots, issue comments, or chat logs.

startup.title: Startup and migration check
body: At startup, the server validates whether Drizzle migrations are applied. If migrations are missing or hashes differ, startup can stop before the UI is usable.
steps: Install dependencies with pnpm through Corepack.
Run migrations with the project-approved command.
Start the dev server and confirm the target port.
Run test and type-check before reporting completion.

commands.title: Standard commands
commands.code:
corepack pnpm dev
corepack pnpm db:push
corepack pnpm test
corepack pnpm check
corepack pnpm format

authStaff.title: Login, Staff Management, and Instance Settings
authStaff.summary: Operational rules for login, setup, staff invitations, roles, language, and instance settings.

login.title: Login and account basics
body: Staff users sign in before using dashboard functions. Public signers do not need staff accounts.
If login loops, first confirm the app URL, session secret, cookie settings, and whether the browser is using the intended hostname and protocol.
bullets: Initial setup creates or enables the first administrator.
Forgot-password and reset-password pages handle account recovery.
The authenticated dashboard redirects unauthenticated staff to login.
Do not share staff accounts between people.
Language can be resolved from user preference, request parameter, or browser settings depending on the surface.

staff.title: Staff and instance settings
body: Administrators keep the staff list, roles, organization settings, mail settings, and integration settings current.

invite.label: Invite
invite.detail: Add only people who should operate the workspace. Use a unique email for each person.

role.label: Role
role.detail: Assign the minimum role needed. Administrator privileges should be rare and reviewed.

deactivate.label: Deactivate
deactivate.detail: Deactivate users who leave the organization. Do not delete evidence that belongs to completed workflows.

locale.label: Locale
locale.detail: Store staff language preference where supported; it is used before browser fallback for staff UI and email.

dashboard.title: Daily Operations and Dashboard
dashboard.summary: Explains daily checkpoints, document statuses, pending tasks, and starting points for common operations.

daily.title: Daily operating checklist
body: The dashboard is the staff starting point for work-in-progress documents and operational alerts.
Use it to find drafts, sent requests, pending approvals, completed documents, and exceptions that need action.
bullets: Check documents waiting for staff action.
Review signature requests that are sent, viewed, or close to expiry.
Open Inbox for tasks assigned to the current staff user.
Create a new document only after the source PDF is final.
Use templates when the same document structure repeats.
Check audit logs before assuming a workflow step did not happen.
Escalate with document ID and timestamp when support is needed.

status.title: Document and signature request statuses
body: Statuses answer what can happen next. They should not be changed casually because API, Inbox, email, and audit behavior depend on them.

draft.label: draft
draft.detail: Document exists but has not been sent. Fields and recipients can still be prepared.

sent.label: sent
sent.detail: Signature request was sent to the next signer or recipient.

viewed.label: viewed
viewed.detail: Signer opened the link. This is evidence that delivery worked, not a signature.

signed.label: signed
signed.detail: A signer completed their assigned signing action.

declined.label: declined
declined.detail: A signer refused. The workflow should stop or follow the configured exception path.

completed.label: completed
completed.detail: All required steps are done and completed PDF/certificate evidence should be available.

expired.label: expired
expired.detail: The signing link is no longer valid and the request needs resend or replacement.

documents.title: Documents, PDFs, and Signature Fields
documents.summary: Explains PDF upload rules, the 20 MB limit, validation, field types, and checks before sending.

pdf.title: PDF upload and validation
body: The source file must be a PDF and must pass server-side validation before fields and recipients are finalized.
The current manual policy uses a 20 MB upload limit. Reject files that exceed the limit or fail PDF validation instead of trying to repair them silently.
bullets: Confirm the PDF is final before uploading.
Do not upload password-protected PDFs unless the implementation explicitly supports them.
Keep page count and file size reasonable for browser signing.
Store the original PDF and generated signed PDF as separate evidence artifacts.
Use PDF proxying for secure viewing instead of exposing raw storage paths.
If validation fails, recreate/export the PDF from the source application.
Do not bypass validation from the UI, API, or CLI.
callouts: A successful upload is not a completed contract. It only creates the source document for the workflow.

fields.title: Signature field types
body: Fields define what a signer must provide and where the result is placed on the PDF.

signature.label: Signature
signature.detail: Drawn or typed signature required from a signer.

initial.label: Initial
initial.detail: Short confirmation mark used when the document requires initials.

stamp.label: Stamp
stamp.detail: Seal/stamp image or equivalent field when business rules require it.

text.label: Text
text.detail: Free text input such as company name, title, or address.

date.label: Date
date.detail: Date field populated by the signer or system according to workflow rules.

checkbox.label: Checkbox
checkbox.detail: Explicit binary confirmation such as consent or acknowledgement.

signing.title: Sending, Signing, Decline, Delegation, and Final PDF
signing.summary: Covers sequential signing, access codes, signing links, decline, delegation, completion certificate, and WORM signed PDFs.

request.title: Sending and signing flow
body: A signature request connects one document, recipients, field assignments, delivery email, access control, and audit records.
Sequential signing sends the next action only after the previous required signer completes their step.
bullets: Set signer order before sending when sequential signing is required.
Use access codes when the recipient identity needs an additional shared secret.
A signing token should be treated as sensitive until the workflow completes or expires.
Signer actions must create audit log entries.
Decline captures the reason and stops or branches the workflow.
Delegation changes who can complete the step and must be recorded.
Completion generates signed PDF evidence and a completion certificate where implemented.
Signed PDFs are WORM-style evidence and must not be overwritten casually.

outcomes.title: Signer outcomes
body: Each outcome has a different operational meaning and audit consequence.

signed.label: Signed
signed.detail: The signer filled required fields and confirmed. Continue to the next signer or completion.

declined.label: Declined
declined.detail: The signer refused. Record the reason and do not treat the document as complete.

delegated.label: Delegated
delegated.detail: The signer transferred action to another person where allowed. Record original and delegated recipient.

completedPdf.label: Completed PDF
completedPdf.detail: Generated final PDF with signature evidence after all required steps finish.

certificate.label: Completion certificate
certificate.detail: Evidence summary such as signer, timestamp, request ID, and audit references.

internalApproval.title: Internal Approval
internalApproval.summary: Explains approval states, approve/reject behavior, and how approvals relate to signature requests.

flow.title: Approval statuses
body: Internal approvals are staff workflows that gate or document an internal decision.
Approval and signature workflows are related but separate; rejecting an approval is not the same event as an external signer declining.

approved.label: approved
approved.detail: The approver accepted. Downstream work may continue according to workflow rules.

rejected.label: rejected
rejected.detail: The approver rejected. Capture reason and stop or return the document to preparation.

cancelled.label: cancelled
cancelled.detail: The approval was intentionally stopped by an authorized staff user.

callouts: Approval decisions must be auditable because they explain why a signature request was sent, stopped, or changed.

actions.title: Approver actions
bullets: Open the approval link or Inbox item.
Review the document, request metadata, and business context.
Approve only when the document is ready for the next workflow step.
Reject with a clear reason when correction is required.
Do not use approval comments for secrets.
Confirm the resulting status before escalating a missing task.

inbox.title: Staff Inbox
inbox.summary: Clarifies that Inbox is not a mailbox; it is a virtual inbox built from existing workflow rows.

concept.title: Virtual Inbox concept
body: Inbox is not an email inbox and it does not store independent mail messages.
It is a virtual view assembled from existing workflow rows such as signature requests, approvals, delegations, and CC visibility.
Because of this design, fixing Inbox issues usually means checking the underlying workflow row and status, not a separate inbox table.
callouts: Do not add a second source of truth for Inbox unless the data model and audit rules are deliberately redesigned.

signature.label: Signature task
signature.detail: A staff user has a document-related action or follow-up.

approval.label: Approval task
approval.detail: An internal approval requires a decision.

delegation.label: Delegation
delegation.detail: A delegated signing or review action points to the current user.

cc.label: CC visibility
cc.detail: The user is informed about progress without being the required actor.

templates.title: Templates
templates.summary: Explains document reuse, field placement, variables, and cautions when changing templates.

usage.title: Template use
body: Templates reduce repeated setup for common documents by preserving layout, fields, recipient patterns, and message defaults where supported.
Changing a template should not rewrite completed document evidence.
bullets: Create templates only from reviewed document patterns.
Name templates by business purpose, not by a temporary file name.
Review fields after duplicating a template.
Keep signer roles understandable for staff and API clients.
Retire obsolete templates instead of silently changing history.
Update the manual when template behavior changes.

document.label: Document variables
document.detail: Title, document ID, creation time, and request metadata.

recipient.label: Recipient variables
recipient.detail: Signer name, email, role, order, locale, and access-code requirement.

sender.label: Sender variables
sender.detail: Staff sender, organization, reply-to, and configured branding.

workflow.label: Workflow variables
workflow.detail: Due date, reminder policy, approval state, completion links, and audit references.

contacts.title: Contacts, Categories, and Groups
contacts.summary: Organizes external recipient management, categories, groups, and recipient selection when sending.

directory.title: Contact management
body: Contacts help staff select recurring external recipients consistently.
A contact is not a staff user and does not imply login access.
bullets: Keep legal names and email addresses accurate.
Use categories for business classification.
Use groups for repeated recipient sets.
Check locale before sending multilingual requests.
Remove or archive obsolete contacts according to policy.
Do not store secrets in contact notes.

category.label: Category
category.detail: Business classification such as customer, vendor, or partner.

group.label: Group
group.detail: Reusable set of contacts for repeated sending patterns.

recipient.label: Recipient
recipient.detail: A specific person assigned to a specific document workflow.

compliance.title: Audit Logs, WORM, and Electronic Books Compliance
compliance.summary: Explains activityLogs, systemAuditLogs, hash chains, PDF proxying, and the no-delete policy.

audit.title: Audit evidence
body: Audit data explains who did what, when, from which workflow, and with which result.
WORM design means evidence should be appended and preserved rather than edited or deleted.
bullets: Record login, document, signature, approval, API, and system-sensitive events.
Use activityLogs for user/workflow activity.
Use systemAuditLogs for system-level and integration-sensitive events.
Hash chains help detect tampering.
PDF proxying prevents accidental exposure of raw storage paths.
Signed PDFs and completion evidence should be treated as immutable.
Deletion policies must preserve required evidence.
If an audit row looks wrong, investigate source logic instead of editing evidence.

worm.title: WORM and no-delete policy
body: Compliance behavior is a product boundary. Avoid convenience changes that weaken evidence retention.

systemAuditLogs.label: systemAuditLogs
systemAuditLogs.detail: Append-only system, security, integration, and operational records.

pdfProxy.label: PDF proxy
pdfProxy.detail: Controlled route for PDF viewing/downloading without exposing storage internals.

hashChain.label: Hash chain
hashChain.detail: Links records so later tampering can be detected.

deletePolicy.label: Delete policy
deletePolicy.detail: Completed evidence is retained. Deleting business records must not erase required audit evidence.

emailLocales.title: Email and Language Settings
emailLocales.summary: Explains email types, UI locales, email-only locales, locale priority, and the meaning of ?lng.

mail.title: Email types
body: Emails are part of the workflow, not just notifications. Missing email can block signing or approval.

invitation.label: Staff invitation
invitation.detail: Sent when a staff account is invited or activated.

signatureRequest.label: Signature request
signatureRequest.detail: Sent to external signers with the signing link and optional access-code instructions.

reminder.label: Reminder
reminder.detail: Sent when a request is pending and reminder policy requires follow-up.

completion.label: Completion
completion.detail: Sent when a workflow completes and evidence is ready.

approval.label: Approval
approval.detail: Sent to internal approvers when their decision is required.

locale.title: Locale priority and ?lng
body: UI locales are ja, en, th, and zh-CN. Some additional locales may be email-only and should not be added to UI selectors unless the UI is translated.
?lng changes the requested language for public links when supported. It is useful for signing links, approval links, and manual verification.
bullets: Prefer explicit ?lng when a public link must open in a specific language.
Use staff preference for authenticated staff surfaces.
Use recipient/contact locale for external emails where available.
Fall back to browser or default locale when no stored preference exists.
Do not change email-only locales when updating UI manual content.
callouts: If raw translation keys appear, compare the manual.* key set across ja, en, th, and zh-CN.

api.title: External Integration REST API
api.summary: Explains API key authentication, scopes, endpoints, idempotency, webhooks, and audit recording.

auth.title: Authentication, scopes, and idempotency
body: The REST API is for trusted integrations. Authenticate with an API key, validate scope, and write audit records for sensitive actions.
Use an Idempotency-Key header for create operations so retries do not duplicate documents or requests.

token.label: API key
token.detail: Send as a bearer token or the project-supported header. Store only in a server-side secret store.

scope.label: Scope
scope.detail: Limits what the integration can do, such as document write, request send, or webhook management.

idempotency.label: Idempotency
idempotency.detail: Required for safe retries of create/send operations.

audit.label: Audit
audit.detail: API actions must record actor, key, scope, target, and result where implemented.

curl.title: Example request
curl.code:
curl -X POST "$SIGN_API_BASE_URL/api/v1/documents" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $SIGN_API_KEY" \
  -H "Idempotency-Key: demo-document-001" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  --data '{"title":"Example contract"}'

createDocument.label: Create document
createDocument.detail: Uploads or registers a source document and returns the document identifier.

createRequest.label: Create/send signature request
createRequest.detail: Creates recipients, fields, access rules, and sends the workflow when requested.

getStatus.label: Get status
getStatus.detail: Returns document/request state for polling or reconciliation.

downloadPdf.label: Download completed PDF
downloadPdf.detail: Returns or redirects to controlled completed-PDF access when the workflow is complete.

webhooks.label: Webhooks
webhooks.detail: Notify external systems about status changes. Verify signatures and retry safely.

cli.title: signctl CLI
cli.summary: Explains environment variables, main commands, output, and idempotency for AI/CI operators.

environment.title: CLI environment
body: signctl is the command-line surface for operators, CI, and AI agents. It should call the public integration boundary instead of writing directly to the database.

baseUrl.label: SIGN_API_BASE_URL
baseUrl.detail: Base URL of the target Hundredth Sign instance.

apiKey.label: SIGN_API_KEY
apiKey.detail: API key with the minimum required scopes.

idempotency.label: SIGN_IDEMPOTENCY_PREFIX
idempotency.detail: Optional prefix to make automated retries traceable.

output.label: --json
output.detail: Use machine-readable JSON output for CI/AI automation.

commands.title: Main commands
body: Exact command names should follow scripts/signctl.mjs. Keep this manual synchronized when commands change.

examples.title: Representative commands
examples.code:
node scripts/signctl.mjs documents:create --file ./contract.pdf --title "Contract" --json
node scripts/signctl.mjs requests:send --document-id doc_123 --recipient signer@example.com --json
node scripts/signctl.mjs requests:status --request-id req_123 --json
node scripts/signctl.mjs webhooks:test --url https://example.invalid/webhook --json

callouts: Do not give signctl a database URL. CLI automation should stay behind the audited REST API boundary.

dataModel.title: DB Table Overview
dataModel.summary: Gives an overview of key tables, state transitions, representative unique constraints, and no-delete data.

tables.title: Primary table groups
body: The actual schema is defined in drizzle/schema.ts. This section is an operator-readable map, not a replacement for migrations.

users.label: users / staff-related tables
users.detail: Staff identity, role, status, locale, and login-related data.

documents.label: documents
documents.detail: Source document metadata, ownership, status, and file references.

signatureRequests.label: signatureRequests
signatureRequests.detail: Sending workflow, status, order, due date, token, and completion state.

signers.label: signers / recipients
signers.detail: Recipient identity, order, access-code flags, locale, and signer outcome.

signatureFields.label: signatureFields
signatureFields.detail: Field type, page, coordinates, required flag, and assigned signer.

approvals.label: internal approvals
approvals.detail: Internal decision workflow and approver outcome.

audit.label: activityLogs / systemAuditLogs
audit.detail: Append-only evidence and security/operational audit trail.

integrations.label: API keys / webhooks
integrations.detail: Integration credentials, scopes, webhook subscriptions, and delivery state.

constraints.title: Constraints and invariants
bullets: Email-like identifiers must be unique where the schema requires it.
Tokens and API keys must be unique and treated as secrets.
A signature field belongs to one document and usually one assigned signer.
A completed workflow must retain PDF and audit evidence.
Status transitions should be explicit and auditable.
Hash chain inputs must not be rewritten after evidence is recorded.
Schema changes require documentation and tests in the same change set.

operations.title: Operations, Troubleshooting, and FAQ
operations.summary: Reduces questions about login, undelivered email, PDF, signing links, API keys, audit logs, migration, and language settings.

faq.title: FAQ by symptom
body: Start with the symptom, then verify the responsible boundary before changing data.

login.label: Cannot log in
login.detail: Check app URL, cookie domain, session secret, account status, password reset flow, and browser hostname.

mail.label: Email not received
mail.detail: Check SMTP settings, recipient address, spam folder, mail logs, locale template, and whether delivery is disabled in development.

pdf.label: PDF upload fails
pdf.detail: Confirm file type, 20 MB limit, password protection, PDF validation error, and server logs.

signingLink.label: Signing link does not open
signingLink.detail: Check token, expiry, request status, access code, route URL, and ?lng parameter.

apiKey.label: API key fails
apiKey.detail: Check key value, scope, target base URL, Authorization header, idempotency key, and audit logs.

auditLog.label: Audit log missing
auditLog.detail: Check whether the action actually occurred and whether the code path inserts activityLogs or systemAuditLogs.

migration.label: Migration error
migration.detail: Check DATABASE_URL, applied migration table, hash mismatch, statement breakpoints, and current schema.

locale.label: Wrong language
locale.detail: Check ?lng, staff preference, recipient locale, browser language, and UI vs email-only locale support.

runbook.title: Operational runbook
steps: Capture screen, route, document/request ID, user or recipient, timestamp, and exact error.
Check whether the issue is UI, API, email, PDF, DB migration, or audit evidence.
Verify environment variables without exposing their real values.
Run the standard test and type-check commands after code changes.
For DB-backed fixes, apply or hand off the migration before claiming operational completion.
Update the manual and product specification when behavior changes.

verify.title: Verification commands
verify.code:
corepack pnpm test
corepack pnpm check
node scripts/verify-manual-locales.mjs # if present in your branch

developer.title: Developer Change Rules and Verification
developer.summary: Rules for keeping specs, DB, audit, translations, tests, and the manual aligned when making changes.

rules.title: Change rules
body: Treat documentation, migrations, translations, and tests as part of the product surface, not cleanup work.
bullets: Read docs/architecture.md and the relevant docs/spec file before implementation.
If drizzle/schema.ts changes, update docs/domain/data-model.md.
Never remove INSERT behavior for activityLogs or systemAuditLogs when it protects WORM evidence.
Do not hardcode secrets; read from environment variables.
Keep UI manual keys aligned across ja, en, th, and zh-CN.
Do not edit email-only locales for UI-only manual changes.
Run pnpm test and pnpm check before completion.
When a feature changes public behavior, update this manual in the same branch.

commands.title: Developer verification
commands.code:
corepack pnpm test
corepack pnpm check
corepack pnpm format

schema.label: Schema
schema.detail: Migration created/applied, data model docs updated, no unsafe data loss.

audit.label: Audit
audit.detail: Sensitive actions write activityLogs or systemAuditLogs and preserve evidence.

locale.label: Locale
locale.detail: No raw manual.* keys in ja/en/th/zh-CN and email-only locales untouched.

manual.label: Manual
manual.detail: Manual reflects product-spec.ja.md and any changed behavior.

test.label: Tests
test.detail: Relevant unit/integration/E2E checks pass or the blocker is documented.

terms.title: Terms for using the OSS edition
terms.description: Operational terms for self-hosting Hundredth Sign OSS. Review these terms together with the repository license and your own legal, security, and compliance requirements.

scope.title: Scope and relationship to the license

body.0: These terms explain recommended operating conditions for the self-hosted OSS edition. They do not replace the repository license, third-party licenses, or a separately signed customization or support agreement.
body.1: If there is a conflict between these explanatory terms and the actual open-source license file, the license file controls software usage rights.
body.2: Organizations that operate, modify, redistribute, or provide the system to others must review copyright notices, license notices, trademark use, and any third-party component obligations.

body.0: The package is distributed as open-source software under the license declared by the repository. The package metadata currently declares MIT, which generally permits use, copy, modification, distribution, sublicensing, and sale if copyright and permission notices are preserved.
body.1: The OSS edition does not include hosted infrastructure, managed service operation, individual troubleshooting, legal confirmation, security certification, or service-level commitments.
body.2: Redistributors and operators are responsible for making their own notices, privacy policy, support terms, and commercial terms clear to their users.

body.0: The operating organization is responsible for accounts, staff authority, API keys, webhook secrets, database, storage, email delivery, backups, monitoring, vulnerability handling, and incident response.
body.1: Before real contracts are handled, verify domain settings, APP_URL, mail delivery, restore procedures, storage retention, PDF download behavior, access restrictions, audit-log integrity checks, and staff offboarding.
body.2: Production operation should define who can create administrators, issue API keys, revoke keys, inspect audit logs, restore backups, and approve emergency changes.
body.3: The OSS edition is not a tenant-switching SaaS product. It is intended to operate as a single self-hosted workspace.

body.0: The visible staff roles in the OSS edition are administrator and member. Administrators manage users, instance settings, audit logs, and API integration keys. Members perform daily document, template, contact, inbox, and signing-related work within their permissions.
body.1: External recipients do not create accounts. They access signing, approval, or document-view pages through token links and any configured access-code or email verification flow.
body.2: Administrators must deactivate staff accounts that no longer need access and should issue the smallest necessary privileges to each person.

body.0: External systems must use the integration API or signctl rather than direct database writes. Direct writes can bypass audit, WORM, PDF completion, status transitions, and email side effects.
body.1: API keys must be stored as secrets, rotated before expiry, scoped narrowly, and revoked immediately when a system, operator, or AI agent no longer needs access.
body.2: Webhook receivers must verify the HMAC signature, handle duplicate deliveries, and avoid treating a delivery as the sole source of truth; Sign remains the source of record.

body.0: The operator controls personal data, signed PDFs, access tokens, signatures, stamps, audit records, email logs, webhook payloads, API-key metadata, and backups stored in the self-hosted environment.
body.1: Define retention, deletion, encryption, access control, backup, restore, export, and disposal policies before business use.
body.2: Review personal-information rules, cross-border transfer rules, subcontractor controls, and breach-response duties that apply to your organization and jurisdiction.
body.3: If real production data is used for development, support, or customization, mask or minimize it unless there is a documented operational need.

body.0: The software records document activity, signer actions, completion certificates, and WORM audit records. These features support evidence preservation but do not automatically prove legal enforceability.
body.1: Electronic signature validity depends on document type, signer identity process, consent, jurisdiction, authentication, retention, and dispute-handling procedures.
body.2: For Japanese tax records and electronic transactions, operators should separately confirm electronic record retention requirements, searchability, authenticity controls, and screen/output readability requirements.
body.3: Consult qualified legal, tax, accounting, security, and compliance professionals before relying on the system for regulated or high-value documents.

body.0: Do not use the system to impersonate another person, forge consent, bypass required approvals, unlawfully collect personal data, distribute malware, or interfere with third-party systems.
body.1: Do not disable audit inserts, WORM retention, token validation, access-code checks, or signed-PDF generation in a way that misrepresents evidence.
body.2: Do not publish API keys, webhook secrets, database URLs, storage credentials, JWT secrets, or private signing certificates.

body.0: The OSS edition does not include ordinary product support, operational support, custom debugging, data recovery, legal review, or service-level commitments.
body.1: Questions may be handled internally by the operator or separately scoped as paid customization, implementation, review, or support work.
body.2: The public OSS distribution is not responsible for diagnosing your infrastructure, mail system, legal process, security posture, database state, or production incident.

body.0: Business-specific customization can be requested separately.
body.1: Typical work includes document formats, approval routes, integrations, language behavior, reporting, deployment, and operational dashboards.
body.2: Customization does not remove the operator’s responsibility to review, test, approve, and operate the resulting system.

body.0: This manual may be updated as the OSS codebase changes. Operators should keep their deployed version, manual version, migration state, and internal operating procedures aligned.
body.1: A separately executed written agreement for paid customization or managed support takes precedence for the scope covered by that agreement.
body.2: The operator should retain a copy of the terms, license notices, and configuration used at the time each production release is adopted.

disclaimer.title: OSS edition disclaimer
disclaimer.description: Read this before production operation, external integrations, or business-specific customization of Hundredth Sign OSS.

body.0: The OSS edition and this manual are provided as is. Availability, correctness, fitness for a particular purpose, non-infringement, legal effect, security, data preservation, and uninterrupted operation are not warranted.
body.1: The operator accepts the risk of evaluating, configuring, testing, deploying, monitoring, and maintaining the system.
body.2: Public OSS code can contain defects, insecure configuration choices, migration requirements, outdated documentation, or behavior that is unsuitable for a specific business.
body.3: The repository license may contain its own warranty and liability wording; read the actual license file before distribution or production use.

legal.title: No legal, tax, accounting, or security advice

body.0: The software, manual, examples, API documentation, and CLI examples are technical materials only.
body.1: They are not legal, tax, accounting, privacy, security, records-management, or compliance advice.
body.2: The final decision about enforceability, retention, evidentiary value, privacy compliance, and regulated use belongs to the operating organization.
body.3: Confirm requirements with qualified specialists in the relevant jurisdiction before relying on the system.

body.0: Electronic signature laws and recordkeeping rules vary by document type, jurisdiction, identity assurance, consent process, retention period, and evidence handling.
body.1: Audit logs, completion certificates, WORM records, and PDF signatures support evidence, but they do not automatically satisfy every legal, tax, or internal-control requirement.
body.2: Some documents may require handwritten signatures, qualified signatures, notarization, special storage, additional identity verification, or a different retention workflow.
body.3: Operators must test whether generated PDFs, logs, timestamps, search fields, and exports remain readable and retrievable for the required period.

body.0: Self-hosting moves security responsibility to the operator. This includes server hardening, patching, network exposure, secrets management, TLS, authentication, IP restrictions, logging, alerting, and vulnerability response.
body.1: API keys, webhook secrets, JWT secrets, database credentials, storage credentials, email credentials, and signing certificates must be protected and rotated when risk changes.
body.2: Backups are useful only if restoration is tested. Monitoring is useful only if someone is responsible for responding.
body.3: AI or CI automation must use narrowly scoped keys, idempotency keys for retries, and explicit approval boundaries for destructive operations.

body.0: Signed documents, signatures, stamps, names, emails, access tokens, logs, and webhook payloads can contain personal or confidential information.
body.1: The OSS distribution does not automatically provide a privacy policy, data-processing agreement, breach-notification process, deletion workflow, or data-subject request process for your organization.
body.2: Data loss, leakage, corruption, misdelivery, mistaken access, or unsupported customization can cause business and legal harm.
body.3: Minimize production data in development and support environments, and ensure exports and logs do not expose more information than necessary.

body.0: The integration API and signctl are powerful operational interfaces. A key with send, download, webhook, or API-key management scope can materially affect production workflows.
body.1: Webhook delivery is best-effort with retries. Receivers must handle duplicates, failures, delayed delivery, signature verification, and reconciliation against the document status API.
body.2: Direct database writes by external systems are outside the supported integration model and can break audit, WORM, status, and PDF completion behavior.

body.0: Parts of this OSS edition may be written or modified with AI assistance.
body.1: AI-assisted development and AI-operated CLI workflows can reduce effort, but they do not remove the need for code review, tests, security assessment, migration review, or operational acceptance.
body.2: Changes to authentication, signing, approval, audit logs, storage, webhook delivery, and compliance behavior require extra care.

body.0: Paid customization can adapt the OSS edition to documents, approval, language, integrations, and internal operations.
body.1: Customization work is scoped separately and should include requirements, security review, migration handling, acceptance tests, and rollback or recovery planning.
body.2: The operator remains responsible for deciding whether the customized result is acceptable for production use.

references.title: Reference information used when drafting this notice

body.0: This notice reflects public reference themes around electronic signatures, electronic record retention, personal-information governance, security operations, and OSS no-warranty language.
body.1: Reference materials can change. Operators should review the current official sources that apply to their business before production use.
body.2: This page is a practical risk notice, not a substitute for the official legal text or professional advice.

ossContact.title: Customize Hundredth Sign OSS for your business
ossContact.description: The OSS edition is free to use, and paid customization is available for organizations that need it to match their operations.

body.0: Hundredth Sign OSS does not include general support for ordinary OSS use.
body.1: If you want the system adapted to your contracts, approval routes, internal controls, multilingual operation, integrations, or back-office workflow, you can request paid implementation support.
body.2: As AI-assisted coding becomes common, systems strongly fitted to each business will become more practical than generic SaaS workflows.
body.3: Please send a concise description of the workflow, current pain points, users, document types, and desired timeline.

requestItems.1: Required custom screens, fields, or integrations

ossContact.emailDescription: Send the workflow you want to customize, current issues, and desired timing to this address.
